infobox.key: Countries sometimes included in West Indies
infobox.value: The **East Indies** (or simply the **Indies**) is a term used in historical narratives of the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"). The *Indies* broadly referred to various lands in [the East](/wiki/Eastern_world "Eastern world") or the [Eastern Hemisphere](/wiki/Eastern_Hemisphere "Eastern Hemisphere"), particularly the [islands](/wiki/Island "Island") and [mainlands](/wiki/Mainland "Mainland") found in and around the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") by [Portuguese explorers](/wiki/Portuguese_explorers "Portuguese explorers"), soon after the [Cape Route](/wiki/Cape_Route "Cape Route") was discovered. In a narrow sense, the term was used to refer to the [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago"), which today comprises the [Philippine Archipelago](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_the_Philippines "List of islands of the Philippines"), [Indonesian Archipelago](/wiki/Indonesian_Archipelago "Indonesian Archipelago"), [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"), and [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea"). Historically, the term was used in the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") to refer to the [coasts](/wiki/Coast "Coast") of the [landmasses](/wiki/Landmass "Landmass") comprising the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") and the [Indochinese Peninsula](/wiki/Mainland_Southeast_Asia "Mainland Southeast Asia") along with the [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Historical term referring to non-Burmese Southeast Asia

"Indies" redirects here. For other uses, see [Indies (disambiguation)](/wiki/Indies_(disambiguation) "Indies (disambiguation)").

For other uses, see [East Indies (disambiguation)](/wiki/East_Indies_(disambiguation) "East Indies (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [East India](/wiki/East_India_(disambiguation) "East India (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Map_of_the_East_Indies_by_Jodocus_Hondius_in_1606.jpg/250px-Map_of_the_East_Indies_by_Jodocus_Hondius_in_1606.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_East_Indies_by_Jodocus_Hondius_in_1606.jpg)

A 1606 map of the East Indies

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/1801_Cary_Map_of_the_East_Indies_and_Southeast_Asia_%28_Singapore%2C_Borneo%2C_Sumatra%2C_Java%2C_Philippines%29_-_Geographicus_-_EastIndies-cary-1801.jpg/250px-1801_Cary_Map_of_the_East_Indies_and_Southeast_Asia_%28_Singapore%2C_Borneo%2C_Sumatra%2C_Java%2C_Philippines%29_-_Geographicus_-_EastIndies-cary-1801.jpg)](/wiki/File:1801_Cary_Map_of_the_East_Indies_and_Southeast_Asia_(_Singapore,_Borneo,_Sumatra,_Java,_Philippines)_-_Geographicus_-_EastIndies-cary-1801.jpg)

An 1801 map of the East Indies

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/East_and_West_Indies.svg/330px-East_and_West_Indies.svg.png)](/wiki/File:East_and_West_Indies.svg)

**East Indies**

Indies ([Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") and [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), see [Greater India](/wiki/Greater_India "Greater India"). Sometimes also including **East Indies**)

[Western New Guinea](/wiki/Western_New_Guinea "Western New Guinea")

[West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies") (present-day; historically included the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") entirely)

**Countries sometimes included in West Indies**

The **East Indies** (or simply the **Indies**) is a term used in historical narratives of the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"). The *Indies* broadly referred to various lands in [the East](/wiki/Eastern_world "Eastern world") or the [Eastern Hemisphere](/wiki/Eastern_Hemisphere "Eastern Hemisphere"), particularly the [islands](/wiki/Island "Island") and [mainlands](/wiki/Mainland "Mainland") found in and around the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") by [Portuguese explorers](/wiki/Portuguese_explorers "Portuguese explorers"), soon after the [Cape Route](/wiki/Cape_Route "Cape Route") was discovered. In a narrow sense, the term was used to refer to the [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago"), which today comprises the [Philippine Archipelago](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_the_Philippines "List of islands of the Philippines"), [Indonesian Archipelago](/wiki/Indonesian_Archipelago "Indonesian Archipelago"), [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"), and [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea"). Historically, the term was used in the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") to refer to the [coasts](/wiki/Coast "Coast") of the [landmasses](/wiki/Landmass "Landmass") comprising the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") and the [Indochinese Peninsula](/wiki/Mainland_Southeast_Asia "Mainland Southeast Asia") along with the [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Indies&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/East_Indies "Special:EditPage/East Indies") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(January 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

During the era of [European colonization](/wiki/History_of_colonialism "History of colonialism"), territories of the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") in Asia were known as the [Spanish East Indies](/wiki/Spanish_East_Indies "Spanish East Indies") for 333 years before the [American conquest](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1898) "Treaty of Paris (1898)") and later the [independence of the Philippines](/wiki/Treaty_of_Manila_(1946) "Treaty of Manila (1946)"). [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Empire "Dutch Empire") occupied colonies in the area were known for about 300 years as the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies") until [Indonesian independence](/wiki/Proclamation_of_Indonesian_Independence "Proclamation of Indonesian Independence"). The East Indies may also include the former [French Indochina](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina"), former British territories [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong") and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") and former [Portuguese Macau](/wiki/Portuguese_Macau "Portuguese Macau") and [Timor](/wiki/Timor "Timor"). It does not, however, include the former [Dutch New Guinea](/wiki/Netherlands_New_Guinea "Netherlands New Guinea"), which is geographically considered to be part of [Melanesia](/wiki/Melanesia "Melanesia").

The inhabitants of the East Indies are never called *East Indians*, as they are not linguistically related to South Asia, most specifically the [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"). It distinguishes them both from inhabitants of the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") (which is also called the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies")) and from the [indigenous peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") who are often called *[American Indians](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States")*. However, in colonial times, the non-Indian Southeast Asians were also called "Indians".

Peoples of the East Indies comprise a wide variety of cultural diversity, and the inhabitants do not consider themselves as belonging to a single [ethnic group](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group"). The region is mostly populated by the [Austronesians](/wiki/Austronesians "Austronesians"), who first expanded from the island of [Taiwan](/wiki/Geography_of_Taiwan "Geography of Taiwan"), and later on during the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"), when East Asians such as the [Han Chinese](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese") started to migrate south and became known as the [Peranakans](/wiki/Peranakans "Peranakans") or Straits Chinese.

[Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") are the most popular [religions](/wiki/Major_religious_groups "Major religious groups") throughout the region, while [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"), [Chinese folk religion](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion") and various other traditional beliefs and practices are also prominent in some areas. The major languages in this area draw from a wide variety of [language families](/wiki/Language_family "Language family") such as the [Austronesian](/wiki/Austronesian_languages "Austronesian languages") and [Sino-Tibetan languages](/wiki/Sino-Tibetan_languages "Sino-Tibetan languages"), and should not be confused with the term [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"), a group of languages spoken in the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"). Speakers of [Austroasiatic](/wiki/Austroasiatic_languages "Austroasiatic languages"), [Tai–Kadai](/wiki/Kra%E2%80%93Dai_languages "Kra–Dai languages"), and [Hmong–Mien languages](/wiki/Hmong%E2%80%93Mien_languages "Hmong–Mien languages") also live in the region.

Regions of the East Indies are sometimes known by the colonial empire they once belonged to, hence, *Spanish East Indies* means the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), *Dutch East Indies* means [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), and *British East Indies* refers to [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia").

Historically, the king of [Abyssinia](/wiki/Ethiopian_Empire "Ethiopian Empire") (modern [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia")) was identified with "[Prester John](/wiki/Prester_John "Prester John") of the Indies", since that part of the world was imagined to be one of "Three Indias".

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_Indies&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/COLLECTIE_TROPENMUSEUM_Rivier_Kali_Besar_in_de_benedenstad_van_Djakarta_West_Java_TMnr_10014936.jpg/250px-COLLECTIE_TROPENMUSEUM_Rivier_Kali_Besar_in_de_benedenstad_van_Djakarta_West_Java_TMnr_10014936.jpg)](/wiki/File:COLLECTIE_TROPENMUSEUM_Rivier_Kali_Besar_in_de_benedenstad_van_Djakarta_West_Java_TMnr_10014936.jpg)

[Batavia](/wiki/Batavia,_Dutch_East_Indies "Batavia, Dutch East Indies"), [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies").

Exploration of the *East Indies* by [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") powers began in the last three years of the 15th century and continued into the 16th century, led by the [Portuguese](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal") [explorers](/wiki/Exploration "Exploration").[[4]](#cite_note-WDL-4) The Portuguese described the entire region they discovered as the *Indies*. Eventually, the region would be broken up into a series of *Indies*: The East Indies, which was also called "Old Indies" or "Great Indies", consisting of [India](/wiki/India "India"), and the West Indies, also called "New Indies" or "Little Indies", consisting of the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

These regions were important sources of trading goods, particularly [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton"), [indigo](/wiki/Indigo_dye "Indigo dye") and [spices](/wiki/Spice "Spice") after the establishment of European trading companies: the [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company") and [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company"), among others, in the 17th century.

The [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World") was initially thought to be the easternmost part of the *Indies* by explorer [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus"), who had grossly underestimated the westerly distance from Europe to Asia. Later, to avoid confusion, the New World came to be called the "West Indies", while the original Indies came to be called the "East Indies".

The designation *East Indian* was once primarily used to describe people of all of the East Indies, in order to avoid the potential confusion from the term [American Indian](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") who were once simply referred to as *Indians* (see the [Native American name controversy](/wiki/Native_American_name_controversy "Native American name controversy") for more information).
